Ninja, I want to build a web application that tracks and compares the latest cloud GPU instance pricing and hardware specifications across major cloud providers, and presents everything in an interactive dashboard.

The app should collect publicly available information from provider websites, pricing pages, and documentation, including AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, Lambda, CoreWeave, RunPod, and <http://Vast.ai|Vast.ai> where reliable public data is available.

For each GPU instance or offering, it should compare information such as:

- Provider
- Instance or product name
- GPU model
- Number of GPUs
- GPU memory
- Total available GPU memory
- vCPU count
- System memory
- On-demand hourly price
- Spot or interruptible price, if publicly available
- Region, if pricing differs by location
- Supported billing model
- Whether the instance is suitable for model training, fine-tuning, batch inference, or real-time inference

Prices from different currencies should be normalized into USD where possible. The dashboard should clearly distinguish directly published data from estimated or normalized values.

I want the dashboard to include:

- A sortable comparison table containing all collected GPU offerings.
- Filters by provider, GPU model, GPU memory, workload type, billing model, and hourly pricing tier.
- A search function for locating a specific GPU or instance.
- Charts comparing hourly prices across providers and GPU models.
- A chart showing price per GB of GPU memory.
- A section comparing single-GPU and multi-GPU offerings.
- A “best value” section recommending suitable options for common workloads, including:
  - Running small open-source models
  - Serving a 70B parameter model
  - LoRA fine-tuning
  - Large-scale distributed training
  - Cheap batch inference
  - Short-term experimentation
- A detail view explaining why each recommended offering was selected.
- A timestamp showing when the data was last collected.
- Source links for every provider or pricing record.
- A screenshot of the final dashboard running locally.

The application should use a clear and maintainable data model so that providers and GPU offerings can be updated or added later without rewriting the dashboard.

You should scrape or extract data from public websites and documentation only. I do not want to provide any API keys, cloud accounts, paid subscriptions, or authenticated access.

Some cloud pricing pages may be dynamically rendered, region-dependent, protected against automated scraping, or too complicated to extract reliably. If a source cannot be reliably scraped:

1. Clearly identify the affected provider or data field.
2. Use public documentation, public pricing tables, or another reliable public source as a fallback.
3. Mark fallback or estimated data in the dataset.
4. Do not fabricate missing prices or hardware specifications.
5. Keep the application functional even when one or more provider sources fail.

Please also create a short README that explains:

- The application architecture
- The data collection process
- The normalized data schema
- How recommendations are calculated
- How to install and run the application locally
- Known data limitations
- How to refresh the pricing data

Before finishing, run the application locally, verify that the filters, sorting, charts, recommendations, and source links work correctly, and capture a screenshot of the completed dashboard.

Please treat this as a large and complex task. You are advised to use the orchestrator to divide the work into smaller tasks, such as source research, data extraction, data normalization, frontend implementation, recommendation logic, testing, and final verification.

THE DESTRUCTION OF SODOM AND GOMORRAH 19 The two angels entered Sodom in the evening as Lot was sitting in Sodom’s gateway. When Lot saw them, he got up to meet them. He bowed with his face to the ground 2 and said, “My lords, turn aside to your servant’s house, wash your feet, and spend the night. Then you can get up early and go on your way.” “No,” they said. “We would rather spend the night in the square.” 3 But he urged them so strongly that they followed him and went into his house. He prepared a feast and baked unleavened bread for them, and they ate. 4Before they went to bed, the men of the city of Sodom, both young and old, the whole population, surrounded the house. 5 They called out to Lot and said, “Where are the men who came to you tonight? Send them out to us so we can have sex with them!” 6 Lot went out to them at the entrance and shut the door behind him. 7 He said, “Don’t do this evil, my brothers. 8 Look, I’ve got two daughters who haven’t been intimate with a man. I’ll bring them out to you, and you can do whatever you want a to them. However, don’t do anything to these men, because they have come under the protection of my roof.” 9“Get out of the way!” they said, adding, “This one came here as an alien, but he’s acting like a judge! Now we’ll do more harm to you than to them.” They put pressure on Lot and came up to break down the door. 10 But the angels b reached out, brought Lot into the house with them, and shut the door. 11 They struck the men who were at the entrance of the house, both young and old, with blindness c so that they were unable to find the entrance. 12 Then the angels said to Lot, “Do you have anyone else here: a son-in-law, your sons and daughters, or anyone else in the city who belongs to you? Get them out of this place, 13 for we are about to destroy this place because the outcry against its people is so great before the Lord, that the Lord has sent us to destroy it.” 14 So Lot went out and spoke to his sons-inlaw, who were going to marry dhis daughters. “Get up,” he said. “Get out of this place, for the Lord is about to destroy the city!” But his sons-in-law thought he was joking. 15 At daybreak the angels urged Lot on: “Get up! Take your wife and your two daughters who are here, or you will be swept away in the punishmente of the city.” 16 But he hesitated. Because of the Lord’s compassion for him, the men grabbed his hand, his wife’s hand, and the hands of his two daughters. They brought him out and left him outside the city. 17 As soon as the angels got them outside, one of themf said, “Run for your lives! Don’t look back and don’t stop anywhere on the plain! Run to the mountains, or you will be swept away!” 18 But Lot said to them, “No, my lords g — please. 19 Your servant has indeed found favor with you, and you have shown me great kindness by saving my life. But I can’t run to the mountains; the disaster will overtake me, and I will die. 20 Look, this town is close enough for me to flee to. It is a small place. Please let me run to it — it’s only a small place, isn’t it? — so that I can survive.” 21 And he said to him, “All right, h I’ll grant your requesti about this matter too and will not demolish the town you mentioned. 22Hurry up! Run to it, for I cannot do anything until you get there.” Therefore the name of the city is Zoar.j 23 The sun had risen over the land when Lot reached Zoar. 24 Then out of the sky the Lord rained on Sodom and Gomorrah burning sulfur from the Lord. 25 He demolished these cities, the entire plain, all the inhabitants of the cities, and whatever grew on the ground. 26 But Lot’s wife looked back and became a pillar of salt. 27 Early in the morning Abraham went to the place where he had stood before the Lord. 28 He looked down toward Sodom and Gomorrah and all the land of the plain, and he saw that smoke was going up from the land like the smoke of a furnace. 29 So it was, when God destroyed the cities of the plain, he remembered Abraham and brought Lot out of the middle of the upheaval when he demolished the cities where Lot had lived. THE ORIGIN OF MOAB AND AMMON 30 Lot departed from Zoar and lived in the mountains along with his two daughters, because he was afraid to live in Zoar. Instead, he and his two daughters lived in a cave. 31 Then the firstborn said to the younger, “Our father is old, and there is no man in the land to sleep a 19:8 Lit do what is good in your eyes b 19:10 Lit men, also in v.12 c 19:11 Or a blinding light d 19:14 Lit take e 19:15 Or iniquity, or guilt f 19:17 LXX, Syr, Vg read outside, they g 19:18 Or my Lord, or my lord h 19:21 Or “Look! i 19:21 Lit I will lift up your face j 19:22 In Hb, the name Zoar is related to “small” in v. 20; its previous name was “Bela”; Gn 14:2. SARAH RESCUED FROM ABIMELECH 20 From there Abraham traveled to the region of the Negev and settled between Kadesh and Shur. While he was staying in Gerar, 2Abraham said about his wife Sarah, “She is my sister.” So King Abimelech of Gerar had Sarah brought to him. 3 But God came to Abimelech in a dream by night and said to him, “You are about to die because of the woman you have taken, for she is a married woman.” c 4 Now Abimelech had not approached her, so he said, “Lord, would you destroy a nation even though it is innocent? 5 Didn’t he himself say to me, ‘She is my sister’? ninja, I want to build a web application that tracks the latest AI model pricing and capabilities across major providers, and puts everything into an interactive dashboard for me.
The app should collect public information from provider websites and documentation pages, including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, xAI, Mistral, and DeepSeek. It should compare things like input token price, output token price, context window size, supported modalities, release date if available, and whether the model is suitable for coding, reasoning, vision, or general chat.
I want the dashboard to include:
1. A sortable comparison table of all models.
2. Filters by provider, model type, context window size, and pricing tier.
3. Charts showing price differences between providers.
4. A “best value” section that recommends models for common use cases, such as coding, long-context analysis, cheap summarization, and multimodal tasks.
5. A timestamp showing when the data was last collected.
6. A screenshot of the final dashboard running locally.
You should scrape or extract the data from public websites only. I do not want to provide any API keys or paid logins. Please tell me if any source cannot be reliably scraped and use reasonable fallback data from public docs where needed.
Please treat this as a big and complex task, which you are advised to use orchestrator to handle it
title: AI Model Price Dashboard

---

## Loop Phase 1 — WORK ONE ISSUE

Ninja uses **GitHub Issues as its work queue**. Work **exactly ONE issue this
cycle** — the single highest-priority open issue. Do not start a second one;
the next cycle (a fresh orchestrator run) will pick up the next issue. Keeping
each cycle to one issue keeps runs small, focused, and recoverable.

1. List the open issues: `python tools/issues.py list`
2. Pick the **single highest-priority** open issue. That is the only issue you
   work this cycle.
3. **Understand it before acting.** Read the full issue (title, body, and any
   comments). Issues are often terse and may lack context, so before starting:
   - Check the issue comments for clarifications.
   - **Read recent Slack history for context** — the issue usually originated
     from a Slack conversation: `python slack_interface.py read -l 50`
     (raise `-l` if you need to go further back). Use it to recover intent,
     constraints, and acceptance criteria that aren't written in the issue.
   - If it's still ambiguous, comment on the issue with your understanding /
     questions rather than guessing.
4. Work that one issue to completion.
5. As you make progress, comment on it: `python tools/issues.py comment <n> --body "..."`
6. When it is fully done, close it with a summary:
   `python tools/issues.py close <n> --comment "done: <what/where, PR # if any>"`
7. **If you cannot complete it** (missing access/credentials, external
   dependency, waiting on a human), do NOT leave it open-and-stuck and do NOT
   close it. Mark it blocked so it leaves the work queue:
   `python tools/issues.py block <n> --comment "why blocked + what is needed"`
   Blocked issues are revisited periodically and rejoin the queue via
   `python tools/issues.py unblock <n>` once the blocker clears.

   invent new work here — only work the one existing issue you selected. Filing
   new issues happens in Phase 2 below. See `agent-docs/LOOP.md`.

pick it up in a controlled, queued way.

texts:
Weekly AI model pricing refresh (run from repo root /workspace/ninja). Run TWO scrapers IN ORDER, then VALIDATE:
1) python apps/model-pricing-dashboard/scrape_pricing.py — scrapes the public aggregator aipricing.guru via Tavily and regenerates apps/model-pricing-dashboard/data/models.json with a fresh last_collected timestamp. It aborts on 0 rows; unmatched models keep their last-known price flagged price_stale.
2) python apps/model-pricing-dashboard/scrape_official.py — drives the persistent browser skill to render each provider's OWN pricing + model pages and overlays confirmed 'official' prices and context windows on top of the aggregator baseline. MUST run AFTER step 1 so official prices take precedence. It matches strictly by exact model name and leaves any model it cannot confirm untouched. It runs TWO non-blocking price-drift guards (each flagging moves >25%): 'Price drift >25% vs previous: N' (official vs the aggregator value) and 'Price drift >25% vs previous run (DATE): N' (run-over-run vs the last snapshot in data/price_history.json, which it appends to, capped at 12 runs).
3) python apps/model-pricing-dashboard/validate_dataset.py --json — validates the assembled data/models.json against the dashboard's integrity invariants. Read the JSON report's "ok" (bool), "errors" (list) and "warnings" (list). ERRORs mean the dataset is malformed (bad provenance, negative/missing price, non-positive context window, a stale row not priced fallback, count/timestamp mismatch, duplicate name). WARNINGs are non-failing smells — chiefly the 'collision smell' (two same-family models sharing an identical price triple, the shape of the #97 mis-match).
The repo auto-commits the change. Then post ONE short summary line to Slack: official matched count (see the official_refresh block / script stdout) + aggregator matched vs stale counts + the new last_collected date + BOTH drift counts + a validation segment formatted 'validation PASS/FAIL · E errors · W warnings'. If validation FAILed (any ERROR), lead with that prominently — do NOT report a clean refresh; list the errors and treat the run as needing review. If there are collision-smell (or other) warnings, call them out even when validation passes. If either drift > 0, call it out as a possible parser/layout regression to review. If either scraper reports 0 rows or errors, say so and do NOT overwrite data on failure (scrape_pricing aborts on 0 rows; scrape_official leaves prices untouched if a page cannot be rendered). Public sources only; no API keys, no paid logins.
title: Weekly AI Model Pricing Refresh

3. **Slack Interface Docs:** `cat agent-docs/SLACK_INTERFACE.md`
4. **Workflow Docs:** `cat agent-docs/ORCHESTRATOR.md`
5. **Integrations:** `cat agent-docs/PIPEDREAM_CONNECT.md` — third-party app integrations via Pipedream Connect gateway and the `pdx` CLI

---

## Current Task

Check Slack #forrest_test_ninja_prod for new requests, do your work, and reflect and improve your toolkit as per agent-docs/ORCHESTRATOR.md.

---

## Loop Phase 1 — WORK ONE ISSUE

Ninja uses **GitHub Issues as its work queue**. Work **exactly ONE issue this
cycle** — the single highest-priority open issue. Do not start a second one;
the next cycle (a fresh orchestrator run) will pick up the next issue. Keeping
each cycle to one issue keeps runs small, focused, and recoverable.

1. List the open issues: `python tools/issues.py list`
2. Pick the **single highest-priority** open issue. That is the only issue you
   work this cycle.
3. **Understand it before acting.** Read the full issue (title, body, and any
   comments). Issues are often terse and may lack context, so before starting:
   - Check the issue comments for clarifications.
   - **Read recent Slack history for context** — the issue usually originated
     from a Slack conversation: `python slack_interface.py read -l 50`
     (raise `-l` if you need to go further back). Use it to recover intent,
     constraints, and acceptance criteria that aren't written in the issue.
   - If it's still ambiguous, comment on the issue with your understanding /
     questions rather than guessing.
4. Work that one issue to completion.
5. As you make progress, comment on it: `python tools/issues.py comment <n> --body "..."`
6. When it is fully done, close it with a summary:
   `python tools/issues.py close <n> --comment "done: <what/where, PR # if any>"`
7. **If you cannot complete it** (missing access/credentials, external
   dependency, waiting on a human), do NOT leave it open-and-stuck and do NOT
   close it. Mark it blocked so it leaves the work queue:
   `python tools/issues.py block <n> --comment "why blocked + what is needed"`
   Blocked issues are revisited periodically and rejoin the queue via
   `python tools/issues.py unblock <n>` once the blocker clears.
8. **Stop after this single issue.** Do NOT start another issue and do NOT
   invent new work here — only work the one existing issue you selected. Filing
   new issues happens in Phase 2 below. See `agent-docs/LOOP.md`.

---

## Loop Phase 2 — REFLECT, PLAN & LEARN (same run, straight after Phase 1)

Once the Phase 1 issue is closed or blocked, continue directly into this
phase — do not end the run after Phase 1. Per `agent-docs/LOOP.md`:

1. **Check Slack** for any new requests that imply work. For anything
   substantial, file a GitHub issue instead of doing it inline:
   `python tools/issues.py create --title "..." --body "..."`
2. **Plan ahead**: based on recent work and the project's goals
   (VISION/spec), file follow-up issues for improvements, fixes, and ideas you
   discovered — so the next cycle has work. Keep them concrete and verifiable.
3. **Build/refine your toolkit**: if you repeatedly need something, add or
   improve a tool under `tools/` (file an issue if it's large).

Do NOT do large implementation work here — capture it as issues so Phase 1 can
pick it up in a controlled, queued way.
